coredata.dc:title: Recent progress on carbon based desalination membranes and carbon nanomaterial incorporated non-polyamide desalination membranes
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yi
Li, Qiang
Tang, Gongwen
Zhang, Na
coredata.dc:description: The membrane based separation technology has been widely applied in water treatment, due to its high efficiency and lower energy consumption. Compared with the traditional polyamide membrane, the novel non-polyamide membrane possesses unique physicochemical structures and surface properties. The introduction of carbon based nanomaterials further endows the membrane with excellent separation performances and resistance to the various feed water and long utilization duration. This paper intends to review the state-of-the-art application of carbon based nanomaterials on the non-polyamide desalination membrane, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), porous carbon and activated carbon. The exclusive properties of carbon based nanomaterials are discussed in the initial section of the review. The novel carbon based free-standing membranes without supporting layer for desalination are comprehensively addressed. The focus is placed on reporting the utilization of carbon based nanomaterials in the fabrication of the different non-polyamide membranes, including reverse osmosis (RO) membrane, forward osmosis (FO) membrane, nanofiltration (NF) membrane, membrane distillation (MD) membrane and pervaporation (PV) membrane. The effects on the non-polyamide membrane performances including water flux, salt rejection, anti-fouling, mechanical properties, and chlorine resistance by incorporating the carbon nanomaterials are detailedly summarized and analyzed. We also discuss the relationship between the membrane microstructure such as chemical modification, porosity and hydrophilicity and key performance mentioned above. Furthermore, the development challenges and research opportunities in this field are also proposed from the aspects of technological challenges, commercialization challenges and environmental impacts/sustainability.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The membrane based separation technology has been widely applied in water treatment, due to its high efficiency and lower energy consumption. Compared with the traditional polyamide membrane, the novel non-polyamide membrane possesses unique physicochemical structures and surface properties. The introduction of carbon based nanomaterials further endows the membrane with excellent separation performances and resistance to the various feed water and long utilization duration. This paper intends to review the state-of-the-art application of carbon based nanomaterials on the non-polyamide desalination membrane, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), porous carbon and activated carbon. The exclusive properties of carbon based nanomaterials are discussed in the initial section of the review. The novel carbon based free-standing membranes without supporting layer for desalination are comprehensively addressed. The focus is placed on reporting the utilization of carbon based nanomaterials in the fabrication of the different non-polyamide membranes, including reverse osmosis (RO) membrane, forward osmosis (FO) membrane, nanofiltration (NF) membrane, membrane distillation (MD) membrane and pervaporation (PV) membrane. The effects on the non-polyamide membrane performances including water flux, salt rejection, anti-fouling, mechanical properties, and chlorine resistance by incorporating the carbon nanomaterials are detailedly summarized and analyzed. We also discuss the relationship between the membrane microstructure such as chemical modification, porosity and hydrophilicity and key performance mentioned above. Furthermore, the development challenges and research opportunities in this field are also proposed from the aspects of technological challenges, commercialization challenges and environmental impacts/sustainability.

ce:keyword.ce:text: ultrathin ( 22–53 nm thick) graphene NF membranes

ce:section.ce:para: To date, over 70% of the earth's surface is covered with river, ocean and glacier in polar region, the problem of clean water shortage is still urgent considering 97% of the water resource is saline and not suitable for direct drinking [1]. The contradiction between supply and demand of purified water will be further worsened due to the aggravation of the water pollution and population increase. The utilization of seawater and brackish water is taking great hope for solving the shortage of fresh-water resources. Many countries have transformed seawater into usable freshwater through desalination technology. The seawater desalination based on the membrane processes contributes almost 53% of the production of drinking water [2]. Nowadays, many desalination technologies have been developed based on various membrane materials, including reverse osmosis (RO) [3], nanofiltration (NF) [4], forward osmosis (FO) [5], etc.

Membrane materials are the key part of the desalting technology and desalination plants in the treatment of brine, brackish water and seawater [6,7]. For instance, polyamide (PA) based thin film composite (PA-TFC) membrane was invented by Cadotte and his coworkers utilizing the interfacial polymerization (IP) process during the 1970s [8]. Since its invention and gradual maturity, PA-TFC used in RO desalting has occupied the leading position in the desalination market with good water purification performance. So far, the conventional polyamide membrane has great progress and widely employed in saline-water desalting as NF and RO materials. With the evolution of membranes, higher requirements are put forward for the performance of the desalting membranes. The ideal performance of the membrane is obtained from high water flux of membrane module while maintaining the salt retention, accompanied by low energy consumption, as well as the properties of anti-fouling and cleanability. To maximize the performance, researchers have attempted constantly to tackle the challenge. For the PA membranes, many defects cannot be avoided, such as the “trade-off” effect between membrane selectivity and water permeability [9], low rejection of small molecules [10], membrane fouling caused by the heterogeneity of PA layer [11], low chlorine resistance caused by the sensitivity of PA layer [12], etc. All of these have hindered the progress of the traditional PA membranes for effective applications.

These practical obstacles emphasize the necessity of rational innovation in membrane design and have stimulated a sustained investigation for novel membrane materials. To address the bottlenecks, researchers have attempted to explore novel materials and molecular-level design approaches for membrane fabrication [13]. Non-polyamide membranes with high thermal stability, anti-fouling and chlorine resistances have become one of the hot spots in desalting membrane research in recent years. Notably, on account of the extraordinary properties of nanomaterials, through manipulating their chemical feature, physical structure and controlling the coupling form between nanomaterials and membrane materials, membranes can be endowed with many preferable properties, such as adsorptive, optical/quantum, catalytic, electrical or anti-fouling properties [14]. Thereinto, carbon based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [15], carbon quantum dots(CQDs) [16], graphene oxide(GO) [17] received special attention from scientific community for their interesting features such as high surface area, special structure and morphology, long range porosity, superior thermal and electrical conductivity, and exceptional mechanical strength and stiffness.

The research on the carbon nanomaterials involved non-polyamide desalination membrane has progressively increased over the last decade. Carbon nanomaterial incorporated non-polyamide membranes have been demonstrated significant improvement in desalination performances and anti-fouling properties. Besides the composite membranes embedded with carbon nanomaterials, carbon based free-standing membranes without supporting layer usually have unique structural and surface properties, and have significant potential in seawater desalination. In this work, both the carbon nanomaterials free-standing and composite non-polyamide membranes for desalination are systematically reviewed. It has not been presented yet in the previous reviews mainly introducing the desalting membrane based on polyamide [18–20]. Moreover, there is no comprehensive overview on the utilization of all types of carbon based nanomaterials in various desalination membranes at present, especially novel non-polyamide membrane materials. This review goes beyond previous work by providing not only the current application of carbon based nanomaterials in untraditional desalination membranes such as RO, NF and other pressure-driven membranes but also a summary of the latest progress in thermal-driven membranes including MD and PV. The reports of carbon nanomaterials utilized in desalination membranes in the past decade for improving the permeability, desalination performances and anti-fouling properties are thoroughly discussed. Finally, future research areas for technology improvement and broader implementation of carbon nanomaterials incorporated non-polyamide desalination membranes are also presented.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic properties of carbon based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The unique properties of carbon nanomaterials can be attributed to the hybridization characteristics. The ground state orbital configuration is 1s22s22p2. The narrow energy gap between the 2s and 2p orbitals facilitates to promote one 2S electron to a vacant 2p orbital with higher energy. The electron promotion makes carbon hybridize into sp, sp2 and sp3 structures, thus forming fascinating and diverse molecular structures [21,22]. The structures make the formation of various carbon materials, such as CNTs, CQDs, graphene, porous carbon, etc. Table 1 shows the properties of CNTs and graphene nanomaterials.

CNTs exhibit a cylindrical hollow nanostructure formed by rolling carbon sheet at specific and discrete angles [23]. The bond type combining carbon atoms in CNTs is very strong. Individual CNTs are naturally arranged into "ropes" held together by van der Waals forces (π stacking). The carbon hexagonal lattice in CNTs leads to electron localization and the charge can move freely [24]. According to the number of walls, two main types of CNTs with high structural perfection are single-walled nanotubes (SWNTs) consisting of a single graphite sheet seamlessly wrapped into a cylindrical tube, and multiwalled nanotubes (MWNTs) comprising an array of such nanotubes concentrically nesting [25].

Graphene, a 2-D allotrope of carbon, is composed of the sp2 hybridization carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal honeycomb structure, which are linked together tightly with strong sigma keys [34]. Graphite oxide (GO) is a layered material made up of the oxidation of graphene [35]. The layer-to-layer distance (d-spacing) of a typical GO is about 0.83 nm measured by X-ray spectrum and the thickness of GO monolayer is 1 nm [17,35]. GO possesses a great amount of hydroxyl and epoxide functional groups located on the basal planes and many carbonyl and carboxyl groups located at the sheet edges [35]. These functional groups make GO sheets strongly hydrophilic and excellent dispersion in water [36]. The hydrophilic nature combined with the high surface area of GO and functional group density, make GO nanosheets can be easily functionalized. [23]. According to the special properties, GO laminates can be easily fabricated by directly assembling individual GO nanosheets, and fabricated to tightly packed interlocking membrane materials [17]. The common methods to prepare GO thin membranes include vacuum and pressure assisted filtration [17], solvent-casting [37], dip/drop casting [38] and spin-coating [39]. In addition, GO laminates can be assembled by the layer-by-layer deposition method deriving from its fantastic features such as the amphipathy, the negatively charged nature, abundant oxygen-containing groups and two-dimensional flexibility [40]. The reduced GO (rGO) is more hydrophobic than GO and can be easily stacked into multilayers [41]. Graphyne is a new carbon allotrope with 2-D structure with both sp and sp2 hybridization [42]. The coexistence of sp and sp2 hybridization and the unique atom arrangement of the graphyne bring many novel properties as a promising material for future electronic, catalysis, and energy applications [43].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of zero-dimensional (0 D) carbon based nanomaterials with various unique properties [44]. They are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles with amorphous to nanocrystalline cores in the range of 3–20 nm in diameter [44]. CQDs usually have functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy that cause good water solubility. Moreover, CQDs are easily available with relatively low production cost and are considered environmental friendly along with biocompatible due to their carbonaceous nature. These properties make it become a promising nanomaterial for membrane fabrication. CQDs as a new class of nanofillers to modify membranes can be applied to the dense-selective layer, the substrate layer and the entire membrane depending on the specific applications.

According to the pore size distribution, porous carbon materials can be classified into microporous carbon with pore size <2 nm, mesoporous carbon with a pore size 2–50 nm and macroporous carbon with a pore size >50 nm [45,46]. Porous carbon is attractive owing to its low-cost precursors, typically biocompatible, high chemically stable, low density, high thermal conductivity, and so on [47]. As one kind of porous carbon, activated carbon (AC) possesses several desirable properties including large surface area and high porosity making them used as a versatile material, which has numerous applications in many areas. AC has been recognized as one type of the most popular and widely used adsorbent for the water and wastewater treatment for removing organic and inorganic pollutants including dyes, pharmaceutical and personal care products, heavy metals, organic pollutants [48–50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Current progress in carbon based membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Hummer et al. first studied the permeability of CNTs in 2001 and indicated how water penetrates through relatively narrow CNTs by the formation of a single "water wires" through CNTs, with water molecules connected by hydrogen bonds [51]. This discovery has greatly encouraged researchers to study the permeability and desalination performance of CNTs. In the stacked graphene membranes, size exclusion is the dominant driving force that determines solute retention. Desalination performance was found to rely on the graphene interlayer distance H and the offset distance. The critical H value is 0.8 nm. Pore offset played an active role in improving salt rejection and the interlayer spacing was large enough to allow water flow while small enough to reject salt ions when H = 8 Å [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental research on carbon based free-standing membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Free-standing ultrathin membranes have been a theoretical and experimental curiosity for several decades because they simultaneously combine the properties of macroscopic materials and colloids along with individual molecules. Free-standing structure could make the thin membrane versatile in practical applications as excluding the interference from the support layer, especially the internal concentration polarization (ICP) inside the support layer. In this chapter, we introduce the research progress of CNTs, graphene and their derivatives as independent membranes in water desalination and purification.

Recently, a flexible film assembled by self-supporting CNTs known as buckypaper has become the main form of free-standing desalting membrane since the preparation of vertical arrangement CNTs is more complex [53]. A buckypaper generally consists of a tangled network of CNT ropes and bundles, as well as some individual tubes, in which CNTs are held together by non-covalent interactions [54]. The young's modulus, thermal conductivity and conductivity can be improved by adjusting the arrangement and orientation of carbon nanotubes [55].

Buckypapers (BPs) have been proposed for a number of applications [56]. Further researches for the potential of BPs as filtration membrane have been investigated [53,57,58]. Rashid et al. fabricated BP membranes from dispersions composed of MWNTs and biopolymers. The mechanical property of the CNTs free-standing membranes was improved because that biopolymer can be adsorbed onto the surfaces of the short nanotubes, and thereby bind them together. It makes them potentially available for the applications of water filtration and solute separation [53]. Furthermore, the desalting performance of MWCNT membranes with chitosan as surfactant in four kinds of salt solutions (NaCl, Na2SO4, MgSO4, and MgCl2) was revealed by Alsuhybani et al. [57]. The membrane had high mechanical properties and the tensile strength was 28 ± 1 MPa. It provided a higher salt rejection for NaCl than that of MgSO4 in high acidic media.

Dumée et al. fabricated CNT BPs membranes with high contact angle (113°), high porosity (90%), and relatively low thermal conductivity of 2.7 kW/m2h by vacuum filtration. Desalination performance was tested in a direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) setup. it shows 99% salt rejection and a flux rate of 12 kg m−2 h−1 at a water vapor partial pressure of 22.7 kPa [58]. Subsequently, they coated PTFE on the top of the CNT-BP membranes inducing higher hydrophobicity prevented water from wicking in the membrane thus potentially reducing the temperature polarization on both membrane sides. Contact angle was increased by 23% and bubble point pressure was increased by 28%. The lifespan and water vapor permeability were improved with salt rejection higher than 99% in DCMD [59]. These researches clearly demonstrate that greater hydrophobicity can lead to better vapor transport. It was likely to be related to the wetting of the membrane surface pores and a thin insulating air film on the membrane surface generated by the hydrophobic forces. These effects were considered to reduce temperature polarization in the boundary layers and heat diffusion within the membrane material [60].

Ahmed et al. developed self-supporting networked cellulose (NC)/carbon nanostructure (CNS) composite membranes. CNS was made up of a network of CNTs formed by branching and crosslinking during chemical vapor deposition. NC/CNS membranes with 50 wt% NC can retain excellent electrical conductivity with a MgSO4 rejection rate of 60%. Due to their high electrical conductivity, these free-standing membranes could mitigate fouling through electrical methods [61].

A 2D porous graphene layer having the pore size ranging from 7.6 nm to 1 mm was fabricated using chemical vapor deposition (CVD). Compared with the commercial membranes, gas, liquid and water vapor permeances were enhanced by several orders of magnitude [63]. Studies have shown that the size of nanopores on graphene is related to the ion selectivity, salt rejection and water transport rate of graphene membrane [64,65]. Yang et al. reported an atomically thin nanoporous membrane designed by an interwoven SWNT network supported single-layer graphene nanomesh (GNM). The SWNT network physically separated the GNM into microsized islands simultaneously acted as the microscopic framework for supporting the GNM. In this way, the GNM structural integrity and mechanical strength can be improved ( Fig. 1). The GNM/SWNT hybrid membranes showed high water flux and rejection to salt ions or organic molecules (up to 95.3%) [62].

The free-standing GO membranes, consisting of 2D GO or rGO nanosheets, are considered the most promising candidate for water desalting application in the future due to their unique structure [66]. Stacking GO nanosheets can be assembled into membranes. The nanochannels formed between the GO or rGO nanosheets are served as selective barriers for allowing gases and ions with smaller sizes to permeate, while blocking all other species larger than the nanochannels [67,68]. Liu et al. reported a facile and efficient approach to fabricating free-standing ultrathin rGO membranes with thicknesses ranging from 200 nm down to 20 nm using hydriodic acid (HI) vapor treatment and water-assisted delamination. FO filtration results indicate that the ultrathin yet robust rGO membrane could not only overcome ICP, but also had high rejection to organic and inorganic species [69]. The narrow space between GO nanosheets is veryimportant to water permeability [70]. Hence, strategies for creating additional nanochannels between intrinsic GO nanosheets have been studied in order to elevate the water permeability, while maintaining a relatively high rejection. Zhang et al. built a photo-assisted multifunctional NF membrane assembled with g-C3N4, TiO2, CNTs and GO. The GO based membrane has high flux (16 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) and ionic rejection (67%) owing to the expansion of the interlayer space between adjacent graphene sheets. Meanwhile, the strong noncovalent π–π interactions between CNTs and GO are also beneficial to enhance the stability and membrane strength [71]. Han and coworkers created continuous nanochannels within GO-based membranes via vacuum filtration. Continuous nanochannels were created by the consecutively distributed TiO2 nanocrystals. Water permeability of the obtained membrane was 7.3 times higher than that of pristine GO membrane [72].

However, practical utilization of multi-stacked GO sheets as desalination membranes is hampered by multiple issues, which include scalability, swelling of interlayer space, and mechanical instability under pressure-driven flux [67,73,74]. The interlayer spacing (d-spacing), defined as the center-to-center distance between two adjacent carbon planes, is critical to determine the separation property of GO membranes [73]. Balapanuru et al. developed a method of laminating partially oxidized few layer graphene (POFG) sheets with an acryl-based sealant to fabricate large-area, free-standing GO-based membranes. The POFG membrane possesses smaller interlayer distance, higher hydrophobicity and swelling resistance. When tested in FO, the membrane shows higher water flux (79 L m−2 h−1) and lower reverse salt flux (3.4 g m−2 h−1) than commercial membranes [75].

Compared with the composite membrane, the carbon nanomaterial incorporated free-standing membranes usually showed better desalination performance. Especially due to the advantage of weak ICP by avoiding the interference from the support layer, they have excellent separation performances. Many studies have proved the effectiveness and potential of the free-standing membranes with carbon based nanomaterials in seawater desalination applications. However, the technology of large-scale production of nanoporous membranes with sufficient mechanical strength has not been completely developed. The swelling of laminated membranes in aqueous solutions has not been conquered yet. They are still major challenges for these membranes using in industrial water treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental research on carbon incorporated composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Polymeric membranes have dominated the commercial applications since 1980 due to the technological maturity, tractability, low cost, and improved permeability and salt rejection [18,76]. Incorporating carbon based nanomaterials into polymeric membranes has been identified as a promising technique, and has arisen the feasibility of developing low-energy, small-scale desalination systems [41]. Thin film composite (TFC) membranes, first developed by Cadotte via IP in the 1970s, consist of a non-woven fabric support, a porous middle polymer layer and a thin (<500 nm) and highly cross-linked dense selective layer [8,77]. Carbon based nanomaterials can be incorporated into either the substrate or the selective layer of TFC membranes, even coating on the surface, which can affect the structure and properties. On the other hand, the MMMs mentioned above, also known as hybrid membrane, combines the unique features of both the polymeric matrix and the inorganic fillers to become a novel and high-performance material. The most commonly used techniques for the preparation of the polymeric membranes include phase inversion [78], interfacial polymerization (IP) [79], stretching [80], track-etching [81], electrospinning (ES) [82] and the layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly [83].

Additionally, inorganic membranes with excellent chemical and physical properties, high tunability and reusability have also gained a growing interest [18]. Typical ceramic membranes are made in multi-layers with different pore sizes, including a thin separation layer and a substrate layer constructed by using metal oxide powders with binders and plasticizers. In a broad term, inorganic membranes consist of ceramic membranes and carbon-based membranes. Oxides such as alumina, silica, titania or the mixtures of these components are some of the commonly used commercialized ceramic membranes [84]. Carbon nanomaterials have also been used as fillers to enhance the ceramic membrane properties.

The pressure-driven membrane process is involved in the process of separating the permeate from the feed solution, with pressure as the driving force [85]. There are different kinds of pressure-driven membranes for desalination, such as RO [3] and NF [4]. Besides, the process of FO is driven by the osmotic pressure difference between a feed solution and the draw solution with high osmotic pressure [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reverse osmosis (RO) membranes with carbon based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: RO offers numerous remarkable benefits such as minimal chemical addition, modular construction，ease of operation and combination with other treatment processes [86]. However, membrane fouling, high energy demand and trade-offs between salt rejection and water flux remain as challenges of RO desalting membranes [9,66]. Endo's group used high-power impulse magnetron sputtering to produce nanostructured carbon based membrane directly on porous polymeric substrates. The optimized carbon based membrane with high resistance to chlorine attack had 96% salt rejection rate for 0.2 wt% NaCl, and about 25 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 water permeability [87]. It has been shown that it is possible to improve permeability and solute rejection of RO membranes by incorporating CNTs with controlled pore size, pore chemistry, surface chemistry and charge [88–90]. Sabir et al. synthesized polymeric matrix based on citric acid /polyethylene glycol 400 (CA/PEG400) which had been tethered with surface engineered MWNT-COOH, and showed that the tethered surface engineered-MWCNT/CA/PEG400 mixing matrix membrane improved the salt rejection and permeate flux at 4.0 bar [91]. Falath et al. fabricated a RO membrane utilizing polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as a polymeric matrix through appropriate crosslinking Pluronic F-127 and MWCNTs. The incorporation of Pluronic F-127 and MWCNTs improved the overall RO membrane performance in terms of hydrophilicity, surface roughness, water permeability, salt rejection, chlorine resistance and biofouling resistance [92]. Furthermore, the electrically conductive RO membranes based on PVA were fabricated by incorporating the networked cellulose and CNTs. The mechanical strength of the membrane was improved, and membrane surface can be recovered after fouling via electrolytic cleaning [93].

Single nanomaterial is incapable of simultaneously offering all beneficial features. However, the hybrid fillers can achieve the desired functionalities in terms of surface hydrophilicity, total porosity, mechanical properties, and desalination performances. Shaban et al. prepared the blend polyethersulfone (PES) membranes by incorporating TiO2-B nanoribbons (TNRs)/MWCNTs via phase inversion method. Cross-sectional SEM images depicted the change in the structure from the spongy-like structure for bare-PES to macro voids structure for blend membrane. The incorporation of TNRs/MWCNTs enhanced the hydrophilicity, mechanical properties, salt rejection and anti-fouling nature [94].

Compared to the CNT with similar pore size, it has been pointed out that the graphene membrane for instant showed a higher water flux through its pores [95]. The formation of holes in GO nanosheets theoretically improves permeability by creating additional routes for water transport. Buelke et al. fabricated GO and hGO (holey graphene oxide) membranes on polycarbonate supports. The hGO membranes demonstrated up to 3.8 times higher permeability than those of GO membranes [96]. Additionally, GO nanosheets were added to CA matrix with the aim of improving seawater desalination performance. It was found that NaCl salt rejection of CA membrane modified with 1 wt% GO at 25 bar was 1.8 times higher than that of the pristine CA membrane, while flux exhibited small reduction [97]. Recently, Zhang et al. developed a dual-functional membrane (PmGn@PVDF) by physically immobilizing GO into the membrane and depositing polydopamine (PDA) on the membrane surface. The obtained PmGn@PVDF exhibited a pure water flux of 188 LMH/bar and an effective Pb (II) treatment volume of 2106.36 L m−2 [98]. Oh and coworkers investigated the pH response behavior of GO membrane supported by hydrolyzed polyacrylonitrile (PAN) in removing ions, which implies the feasibility of making a membrane with oppositely charged layers to simultaneously remove both charged organics and multivalent ions [99]. Endo's team prepared hybrid layered membrane by spray coating an aqueous dispersion of GO and few-layered graphene/deoxycholate onto polysulfone (PSf) support. The membranes maintained NaCl rejection near 85% for 120 h under intense cross-flow [100]. Considering the potential of commercialization, a gradient membrane was designed and assembled with GO and celluloses laminates recently. The nanocomposite membrane was demonstrated a water yield of 21.34 L h−1 m−2 bar−1 with a salt rejection rate of 96.08%, which was 5 times higher than that of current commercial PA membrane. This membrane was indicated a 35.8% energy saving on membrane filtration for brackish water and the cumulative cost savings for Texas was estimated to 7.2 million in 2030 in brackish water RO desalination [101].

The main method of fabricating carbon nanomaterials based RO membrane is phase inversion. A small amount of carbon nanomaterials is commonly added into the organic or inorganic membrane matrix to improve the water flux or salt rejection of the membranes. Information about the usage of carbon based nanomaterial incorporated RO membranes has been shown in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanofiltration (NF) membranes with carbon based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Key distinguishing characteristics of NF membrane are the low rejection of monovalent ions, and high rejection of divalent ions and higher flux compared to RO membrane. The filtration capacity can be enhanced extremely by incorporating carbon nanomaterials with special surface properties in NF membranes. Wang et al. studied the influence of the pore size, membrane thickness and functionalization of CNTs on water transport characteristics under NF operation [105,106]. The positive charged NF membranes with excellent retention performance of multivalent cation have been investigated by researchers. The positively charged NF membranes were prepared by incorporating amine based compounds including polyethyleneimine (PEI), triethylenetetramin (TETA) and hyperbranched polyethyleneimin (HPEI) in MWCNTs. The performances of as-prepared membranes including hydrophilicity, isoelectric point, thermal and mechanical stabilities, anti-fouling property and life span were enhanced. The resulting membranes showed superior separation performance including high permeate flux and salt rejection [107–109].

Introducing hydrophilic functional groups into the CNT surface and attaching polar groups or hydrophilic monomers to the sidewalls of CNTs have been widely used for improving the dispersion of CNTs in polymer matrix, which contributes to the increase of the permeability of NF membranes [110–112]. Carboxylic MWCNTs were doped into poly(m-phenylene isophthal amide) (PMIA) for improving the homogeneity of the dispersion in polymer solvent, which enhances the adhesion of CNTs to polymers, and improving the water flux [113]. Daraei et al. investigated the PES NF membrane with enhanced anti-fouling properties and higher permeate flux by adding polyacrylic acid (PAA) modified MWCNTs [114].

A major limitation of NF membranes is flux decline due to membrane fouling. The hydrophilic surfaces can effectively bind water molecules and generate a water molecule layer on membrane surface to retard the foulants. Vatanpour et al. prepared a series of PES membranes blending with functionalized MWCNTs or TiO2 coated MWCNTs. The c characteristics of the membranes were improved due to the hydrophilicity increase and the decrease in membrane surface roughness [115–117]. ZnO was also used to coat MWCNTs, then mixed with PES casting solution to prepare PES MMMs, which improved the membrane anti-fouling performance [118]. Whereas hydrophobic surfaces repel the water molecules and decrease the contact area between the membrane surface and water, thus minimize the chance of biofouling reaching the surface [119]. Madaeni et al. fabricated a superhydrophobic NF membrane by depositing MWCNTs on the surface of the polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane followed by polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) coating. Almost all MWCNTs were vertically oriented in the membrane surface and provided higher surface roughness leading to an enhancement in hydrophobicity. Superhydrophobicity diminished the interaction between the feed solution and membrane surface and provided superior anti-fouling property [120].

Han et al. fabricated an ultrathin (22–53 nm thick) graphene NF membranes (uGNMs) on a microporous substrate using chemically converted graphene ( Fig. 2). The pure water flux of the uGNMs was 21.8 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. The uGNMs showed 99% retention for organic dyes and 20–60% retention for ion salts. The mechanism of the rejection process of uGNMs NF membranes was revealed as both the physical sieving and the electrostatic interaction [121]. Furthermore, Qin et al. developed high-quality single-layered graphene membranes as ultrafast NF membranes using a large-area in situ phase inversion casting technique [122]. However, there is still a lack of reliable technology for manufacturing large-area and high-quality ultrathin graphene films. It hinders their commercial application.

The well-stacked GO nanosheets exhibit hydrophilic property and negatively charged and hydrophobic nanochannels for water molecules rapidly flowing across the membrane [123]. Surface functionalization on GO film is a very useful approach for tuning the membrane performance, especially separation efficiency. Yuan et al. fabricated GO-COOH membranes for investigating the carboxylation effect on membrane performance. GO-COOH membranes achieved maximum rejections of 48.2% and 91.3% towards NaCl and Na2SO4 at 1.5 MPa. The GO-COOH membranes obtained higher permeate fluxes and better salt rejections compared to the pristine GO membranes. Improved electrostatic repulsion could be the preferential factor that results in GO-COOH membranes possessing prominent separation properties [124]. Meanwhile, an investigation on removal of heavy metal ions from synthetic contaminated water was carried out on a laboratory-scale using a polyphenylsulfone (PPSU) NF membrane incorporated by carboxylated GO. The maximum removal rates were >98% and ~80% for the anions and cations with a higher volumetric flux (27 ± 3 Lm−2 h−1) than the pure PPSU membrane [125]. Abdi et al. fabricated a graphene-based hybrid membrane through amination of GO nanoparticles. The pure water flux significantly increased resulting from the changes in surface roughness and hydrophilicity caused by embedding GO hybrid. Besides, the removal ability of copper and dye was remarkably enhanced attributing to the existence of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of GO hybrid membranes [126]. NF membranes were prepared by GO surface modification utilizing chitosan. The functioned GO membranes have been testified to improve the desalting properties containing higher hydrophilicity, lower surface roughness, higher water fluxes and salt rejections compared with the present commercial NF membranes [127,128].

The nanochannels between graphene nanosheets have significant effect on permeation flux and mass transfer resistance. Coupled inorganic or organic nanomaterials with the GO based sheet membranes may tune the channel size for optimizing permeation and separate performance. The embedded component also can further improve the mechanical property of channels formed by the overlap of the graphene sheets and avoid collapse of the lamellar structure. To control the interlayer distance and enhance the stability of GO membranes, various spacers have been proposed to intercalate into or cross-link with GO sheets, such as Ag nanoparticles, MoS2 nanoparticles and magnesium silicate [129,130]. Gao et al. made hybrid GO layer on the surface of poly(phenylene sulfide) (PPS) microporous membrane by using organosilica to crosslink and/or intercalate into GO sheets. Abundant oxygen-containing groups were introduced into the interlayer of GO nanosheets to form hydrophilic nanochannels and large free interlayer space, result in the enhancement of the permeation and transmission to hydrophilic solvent [131]. Wang et al. prepared a nanodiamond-pillared GO (NPG) membrane via intercalating nanodiamond (ND) into GO interlayer by using vacuum-assisted self-assembly. The intercalation of ND nanoparticles enlarged the interlayer space and created more cavities and channels. The NPG NF membrane was demonstrated with a higher separation ability based on charge discrimination, a faster filtration rate, and consistency of the permeation rate for multivalent cations with the same charge [132]. The interlayer spacing of full reduced GO is 0.36 nm, which is too small to allow water flow through the channels between GO sheets [13]. The interlayer spacing of rGO-based laminar membranes can be tuned by intercalated nanomaterials such as CNTs to increase the membrane pore size so as to enhance the permeability. Besides, the surface charge of the membrane can be increased by grafting abundant oxygen containing functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxide groups) onto the surface of CNTs using chemical oxidation. Consequently, the electrostatic interaction was enhanced between the membrane and charged species. The electrostatic interaction can endow NF membrane with the ability to reject ions or charged molecules. Therefore, CNTs intercalated rGO laminar NF membrane favor enhancing both permeability and selectivity through controlling the membrane surface charge, pore size and interlayer spacing [133–136]. Zhang et al. fabricated oxidized carbon nanotube (OCNT) intercalated rGO membranes by vacuum filtration. The surface charge and the pore size of the membranes were increased with the raise of OCNT addition from 0% to 83%. The permeability and rejection rate were respectively 11.8(10.6 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) and 1.3 (78.1%) times higher than pristine RGO membrane for Na2SO4 filtration at a transmembrane pressure of 2 bar. And for NaCl solution, the permeability of composite membrane was increased by 11.1 times (11.1 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) and rejection rate was increased by 2.9 times (35.3%). This phenomenon provides the possibility of synergistic enhancement in both permeability and ion selectivity [137]. Likewise, the potential to break the trade-off effect between permeability and selectivity has been investigated by an electroconductive three-dimensional hybrid membrane using rGO and CNTs [138]. To enhance the mechanical stability and chlorine resistance, Morelos-Gomez et al. presented a graphene-based membrane by spray coating GO/few-layered graphene and deoxycholate aqueous dispersion onto the PVA-modified PSf substrate [100].

Metal organic framework (MOF) membrane, constructed by metal-clusters connected with organic bridges, has also attracted extensive interest due to their large specific surface area, controllable properties, chemical functional groups and tunable pore structure [139]. Zhang and colleagues intercalated UiO-66-(COOH)2 into partial rGO(prGO) layers to form hybrid structures and changed the interlayer spacing in prGO sheets by intercalating UiO-66(COOH)2 crystalline particles. The pure water flux of UiO-66-(COOH)2/prGO membrane (20.0 ± 2.5 L m−2 h−1 bar−1)was 2.9 times higher than that of pure prGO membrane. The rejection rates for Cu2+ and Cd2+ were 96.5–83.1% and 92.6–80.4%, respectively [140]. Lately，they constructed the substrate by doping GO sheets into PAN casting solution and modified by PDA, and the UiO-66 selective layer on the substrate was formed by vacuum assisted filtration. The pure water permeability of the UiO66/PGP TFC membrane as-prepared was 31.33 ± 0.75 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 [141]. Likewise, Rao et al. prepared MOF/GO composite membrane (IRMOF-3/ GO) with high adsorption capacity of copper (II). IRMOF-3/GO-1 was decorated onto PDA-deposited substrate to form a barrier layer with selective copper ions rejection. The rejection of copper (II) reached up to ~90%, meanwhile maintaining a relatively high flux of ~31 L m−2 h−1 [142].

For GO based TFC NF membranes, except the traditional vacuum filtration and phase transformation approaches [130,135,143]. LbL assembly technique has also been extensively utilized to construct TFC membrane [144,145]. Chen et al. reported a multilayer NF membrane composed of large-area (20 × 30 cm2) GO and nylon 6 nanofibers (GO@ nylon 6) by an LbL assembly process using electrospinning and electrospraying techniques. The GO layers were bound and immobilized by nylon 6 nanofibers networks, and the thickness of the GO layer was tunable by controlling the time of electrospraying. The results demonstrated that GO@nylon 6 composite membranes have excellent filtration performance with improved mechanical stability and water flux [146]. Furthermore, Chen and coworkers prepared a GO NF membrane by electrospraying technique. The GO sheets stacked with each other to form nanocapillaries, hydrophilic edges and defects (holes), which lead to a high water flux up to 11.13 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 [147]. Recently, a low-cost, fast, and scalable method for depositing GO on polymeric supports was developed. Fathizadeh et al. successfully printed a 15 × 15 cm2 ultrathin (7.5–60 nm) uniform GO membrane by inkjet printing on a modified PAN support. The printed GO membranes showed approximately one order of magnitude higher water permeability and small organic molecules rejection than those of the commercial NF membrane [148].

0-D carbon nanomaterials (CQDS and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs)) are promising candidates for the preparation of composite membranes with good hydrophilic, mechanical and antibacterial properties [149,150]. The particular size, shape, and edge structure endow GOQDs with an excellent dispersion in the polymer matrix, which ideally improves the NF membrane performance [151]. Zhang et al. reported the utilization of GOQDs as fillers in thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes for NF process. GOQDs nanosheets were synthesized by the “bottom-up” method of pyrolyzing citric acid (CA) and then incorporated into a tannic acid (TA) layer via interfacial polymerization to fabricate TA/GOQDs TFN membranes. With an increasing concentration of GOQDs, hydrophilicity, roughness, and ζ-potential of the TA/GOQDs TFN membranes were simultaneously enhanced. The short flow paths formed by GOQD nanosheets play important role in water permeation. The resultant permeate flux was increased under 0.2 MPa was from 15.81 to 23.33 L m−2 h−1 [152]. Zhao et al. prepared a GO-based NF membrane with unique homostructure using 0-D GOQDs and/or 2-D GO sheets via vacuum filtration. GOQDs effectively facilitated the passage of water molecules through the membranes. When the GOQDs content was 40 wt%, the water permeability of membranes was increased 3~4 times without compromising their retention for the target solutes [153]. A mixed matrix NF membrane was developed by doping carbon dots (CDs) into a PES matrix by Koulivand et al. The permeability of CDs doped membranes (76.5 kg m−2 h−1) was higher than the bare PES and the membrane containing 0.50 wt% of CDs. Moreover, the fouling resistance of the CDs incorporated membranes was enhanced due to the increase on the surface hydrophilicity. The values of total and irreversible fouling were decreased from 54.7% to 35.6% for the bare PES and from 46.3% to 20.6% for the 0.50 wt% CDs modified PES [154].

In the recent reports, the commonly used preparation process of carbon nanomaterial incorporated NF membrane includes vacuum filtration and phase inversion. The main membrane substrates are polymer materials such as PVDF. Information about the usage of carbon based nanomaterials in NF membranes was shown in Table 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Forward osmosis (FO) membranes with carbon based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The cylindrical confinement in CNTs offers high salt rejection in FO that lays the ground for the ultimate design of FO membranes with optimized performance trade-off [162]. Applications of CNTs in the preparation of FO membranes were mostly investigated from incorporating CNTs in active layer to fabricate TFN membranes. Song et al. prepared double-skinned FO membrane by interfacial polymerization of dopamine/CNTs with TMC on PSf. The double-skinned TFN membrane with appropriate amount of CNTs possesses higher porosity and denser selective layer, resulting in decreased ICP and higher osmotic water flux. The TFN membrane showed a 54% enhancement in water flux than TFC membrane [163]. Zou et al. coated zwitterion functionalized CNTs (Z-CNTs) onto a commercial TFC membrane. Better mitigation of reverse solute flux (RSF) was achieved when coating Z-CNT on the active layer, rather than on the support layer. With an optimal coating density of 0.97 g m−2, a reduced specific RSF was observed for various draw solutes, and forward solute flux (FSF) was notably reduced with fewer pollutants leaked to the draw solution [164]. Cui et al. synthesized functionalized MWCNTs blended cellulose acetate (fCNT-CA) membranes for FO through phase inversion. The fCNT-CA membrane showed more hydrophilic due to carboxylic groups, resulting in approximately 50% higher water-permeated flux than bare CA membrane. The fCNT contributed to improving alginate fouling resistance in FO, due to enhanced electrostatic repulsion between the membrane surface and the alginate foulant [165].

Recently, a 1.2 nm thick carbon nanomembrane (CNM) made from terphenylthiol (TPT) self-assembled monolayers was reported. TPT CNMs possessed rapidly passing of water molecules benefit from their extremely high pore density of 1 sub-nm channel per nm2, while hindering of ions including protons. CNMs showed two orders of magnitude faster water transporting than commercial FO membrane, and the salt concentration in draw solution decreased from 1 to 0.3 M within 60 min [166] ( Fig. 3). Dabaghian’s group fabricated the MMMs using carboxylate and amine functionalized carbon nanofibers (CNFs) to improve hydrophilicity. The osmotic water flux of the amine functionalized CNFs membrane reached 18 L m−2 h−1, which is above 2 times higher than pristine cellulosic membrane. The reverse salt flux of the amine functionalized CNFs membrane was less than 0.5 g m−2 h−1, which is lower than that of the commercial cellulose triacetate (CTA) membrane [167,168].

The appearance of unintended defects is a significant problem for large scale production of graphene membranes. A bi-functional PSf membrane formed on graphene by phase inversion, which was able to duplicate the rough morphology of Cu foil, minimized the formation of graphene wrinkles and unintended defects. The pore sizes of graphene and the transport of ions across the membrane were tailored by adjusting the duration of H2 plasma treatment. The membrane with 90s of plasma treatment exhibited the highest selectivity. The selectivities of KCl over NaCl, KCl over MgCl2, and NaCl over MgCl2 were 2.61, 15.62 and 5.98, respectively [169]. As for GO laminates, the control of layer spacing is still the focus and challenge of investigators. Jin et al. fabricated a GO TFC membrane by integrating GO nanosheets into highly cross-linked polymer network supported by a nylon membrane substrate. The interlayer spacing of GO in the TFC membrane was 0.48 nm and the polymer network entwined GO film was less than 40 nm thick. This membrane showed a water flux of 25.8 L m−2 h−1 and a NaCl rejection of 99.9%, along with mechanical stability and chemically durability for the FO process [170]. In another report, the GOQDs nanoparticles helped in stitching the edges of GO sheets by covalent cross-linked structures. It provided a dense structure between the GO layers and a narrow interlayer spacing by simultaneously improving the capillary interaction [171]. A narrower interlayer spacing of 3.54 Å was observed in the nitrogen-doped graphene membrane, which achieved a specific reverse salt flux of 0.18 g/L, compared to commercial membrane (0.55 g/L) [172]. On the contrary, a cationic surfactant cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (C16TAB) was incorporated between GO sheets of the laminated membrane for expanding the interlayer spacing to 3 nm. It results in higher water flux (20.0 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) for GO-Surfactant membranes as compared to 1.5 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 for pure GO membranes [173]. In a recent research, ethylenediamine (EDA) and PAA were employed together as cross-linking agents into GO membrane. A substantial increase in the d-spacing was observed when both EDA and PAA were added together. The GO/EDA/PAA 8.1:0.9:1 membrane exhibited a water flux of 34.9 LMH while maintaining a reverse salt flux of 0.12 mol m−2 h−1 [174]. Besides, cationic particles were added in order to tune the interlayer space of the GO nanosheets [175]. The nanochannels can be narrowed by chemical reduction of GO by removing hydrophilic functional groups [176,177]. It was reported that the higher the reduction degree, the smaller sized gaps were formed [177]. Adding a hydrophilic PDA coating onto the rGO surface could further improve the water flux by enhancing the water absorption rate into rGO laminates [176]. Additionally, the CNT membrane was reported as an alternative SL for developing FO membranes with an rGO-based active layer. The CNT hollow fiber substrate with high porosity and good wettability weakens the ICP. The water flux of membrane was 40.4 ± 3.7 LMH and salt rejection was about 94.0 ± 1.9% [178]. Kim et al. fabricated the GO entwined by poly(N-isopropylacrylamide-co-N,N′-methylene-bisacrylamide) TFC membranes supported by nylon membrane substrate. The interlayer spacing of GO was 0.48 nm and the thickness of GO–polymer layer was less than 40 nm thick. The resulting TFC membranes enabled water flux of 25.8 L m−1 h−1 and salt rejection of 99.9% along with the excellent chlorine tolerance enduring high chlorine exposure at 1000 ppm chlorine for 24 h [170].

Conventional support layers of FO membranes are comprised of a tortuous 1-D architecture, which functions as a stable barrier against the diffusion of draw solute and water molecules [179]. Qin et al. found that constructing a GO-polymer 3D architecture with interconnected pores in the membrane support layer was an effective approach to overcome the ICP bottleneck. The FO membrane with GO-polymer 3-D architecture showed the water flux of 18.3 ± 1.5 L m−2 h−1 (salt rejection 94.8% ± 1.2%), which is 73% higher than the membrane with the conventional 1-D architecture and 280% higher than the commercial HTI CTA FO membrane [180]. Information about the usage of carbon based nanomaterials FO membranes has been shown in Table 4. The desalination performances of various pressure-driven carbon based desalination membranes are displayed in Fig. 4. The comparison of rejection performance vs. the permeate flux of some RO, NF and FO membranes can be directly observed.

The principle of thermal-driven desalting process is mainly based on the phase transition caused by energy addition or removal to separate fresh water from saline water [186]. The desalination technology that combines a thermal phase change with a membrane includes membrane distillation (MD) and pervaporation (PV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane distillation (MD) membranes with carbon based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The structural characteristics of MD membrane determine the resistance to mass and heat transfer, including high hydrophobicity, high porosity, uniform pore size and narrow pore size distribution, low tortuosity, as well as thin thickness [187]. Mitra’s group has demonstrated in the last decade that immobilizing CNTs in different types of membranes, which is referred to as carbon nanotube immobilized membrane (CNIM), enhanced MD desalting efficiency [188–191]. They observed that CNIM shows higher flux and salt reduction compared to conventional MD. CNIM membrane can decrease the operation temperature, enhance the flow rate, and decrease the salt concentration [192]. They furtherly investigated the application of CNIM membrane comprised of carboxylated CNTs in DCMD process. The incorporation of CNTs-COOH into the hydrophobic membrane conductively changed the interaction between water and membrane to enhance vapor permeability while preventing liquid from penetration into the membrane pores. The maximum permeate flux was 77 kg m−2 h−1 and salt reduction rate was higher than 99.9% [193]. More than that, Mitra et al. developed a bilayered structure with the hydrophobic CNT-octadecylamine on the feed side of membrane and hydrophilic CNT-COOH on the permeate side of membrane. The hydrophobic surface of membrane provided higher water vapor permeation, while the hydrophilic side offered a surface for the condensation of water vapor. The flux of the bilayered membrane reached 126 kg m−2 h−1 owing to the high mass transfer coefficients [194]. Silva et al. fabricated the PVDF flatsheet membranes blended with MWCNTs by the phase inversion method for DCMD. The microstructure of the membrane was characterized by large pore sizes, high pore density and spongy pores with the increasing load of pristine CNTs up to an optimum of 0.2 wt%. And the water flux was 9.5 × 10−3 kg m−2 s−1 while maintaining a complete salt rejection (i.e. 100%) after 60 min [195].

Like all other membrane processes, fouling is one of the major issues affecting the long-term stability of MD membrane. Hence, the MD membrane must consist of at least one hydrophobic layer which will not be wetted by the feed liquid [196]. Dong et al. presented a superhydrophobic ceramic−based CNTs membrane by quantitative regulation of in situ grown CNT networks. The distinct surface structure provided a superporous and superhydrophobic interlayer greatly reducing the contact area between feed water and membrane surface. It also enlarged the water-vapor interface to increase the MD flux without membrane wetting [197]. Sun et al. engineered a defect-free carbon nanotube forest (CNTF) in situ on a porous electrospun silica fiber substrate. The CNTs grew upright on the surface of substrate forming a radially aligned vertical CNTF. The CNTF architecture was optimized by tuning the orientation, density and height of the CNTF. The engineered CNTF created an ultrarough and porous interface structure, showing outstanding wetting resistance against water in air and oil underwater. Owing to that, the composite membrane with a relatively long vertical CNTF performed higher water flux in MD desalination. The composite CNTF membrane displayed a stable water vapor flux and a salt rejection nearly complete ( Fig. 5) [198]. The CNT composite membranes are demonstrated robust fouling resistance and can be reused [198,199]. Owing to the unique electrical property of CNTs, porous, electrically conducting composite membranes have been fabricated. These membranes possess exceptional anti-fouling and self-cleaning properties when an electrical potential is applied on the membrane surface [200–202]. Li et al. applied an electrical potential on the surface of conducting PP membrane coated with CNTs, resulting in water electrolysis and hydroxide ion formation. With the elevation of pH value, the flux of membrane rapidly restored due to the dissolution of silicate gel [201]. Recently, Rao et al. reported an anti-scaling method applying alternating currents to the membrane surface for minimizing CaSO4 scaling. An electrically conducting MD membrane was fabricated by depositing a percolating network of CNTs onto a hydrophobic porous substrate [203].

Electrospun nanofiber membranes have attracted increasing attention in MD process because of their high porosity and interconnected open pores. Theoretical approaches have been conducted to prove the mechanism of CNTs composite electrospun membrane enhancing the DCMD performance [204]. Tijing et al. prepared polyvinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene (PcH) nanofiber membranes by electrospinning using CNTs as nanofillers, to elevate the mechanical strength and hydrophobic properties. The electrospun membrane was designed into two cohesive layers, including a rough and thin CNT/PcH top layer leading to superhydrophobic surface and a thick and neat PcH bottom layer. When 5 wt% CNT was incorporated into the nanofibers, the contact angle increased to superhydrophobic of 158.5° [205].

Compared with common microporous polymer membranes, the simulation results showed that the permeate flux of the mesoporous graphene membrane was three orders of magnitude higher [206]. Therefore, there are a large number of hydrophobic composite membranes composed of graphene/PVDF and its derivatives/PVDF emerged for air gap membrane distillation (AGMD) and DCMD [207–212]. These membranes were prepared by phase inversion or electrospinning. In contrast with the pristine PVDF membrane, the composite membrane with the optimal graphene loading showed good wetting resistance, chemical stability, high water flux, better rejection performance, and less scaling tendency. Leaper et al. reported the fabrication of PVDF MMMs incorporated with 3-(aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTS)-functionalized GO for MD applications. The best performing membrane contained 0.3 wt% GO-APTS which shows a permeate flux of 6.2 LMH whilst maintaining salt rejection >99.9% [213]. Su et al. synthesized the GO-PVP/PVDF membranes by connecting GO sheets to PVP. This membrane not only allows the moisture to pass through the overlapping graphene gaps but also the PVP cracks. The GO-PVP/PVDF membrane demonstrated high permeability with the vapor diffusivity of 1.82 × 10 m2/s, and high hydrophobicity with the contact angle of 145.2° [214]. Li et al. fabricated the fluorooctyltriethoxysilane (FTES)-GO incorporated PVDF nanofiber composite layer on the surface of PP nonwoven fabric via electrospinning. The surface hydrophobicity, liquid entry pressure (LEP) and water permeability of the membrane were improved. The water contact angle increased to 140.5° and the permeation water flux reached a maximum value of 36.4 kg m−2 h−1 with 4 wt% FTES-GO content [215]. Lu et al. introduced n-butylamine (NBA) modified GO (GO-NBA) into PVDF membranes. It was found that with a low loading of 0.5 wt% GO-NBA in PVDF hollow fiber membranes, the maximum tensile stress, maximum tensile strain and Young's modulus increased by 32%, 63% and 71%, respectively. While the LEP and burst pressure jumped by 67% and 15%, respectively [216]. For increasing the economic viability and application potential in the MD desalination process, a self-heated VMD system employing a graphene–PVDF membrane Joule heater can be employed to overcome the temperature polarization and energy inefficiency [217].

Seo et al. investigated the anti-fouling effects of the graphene-based membrane by molecular dynamics simulation. They observed that the channels between the mismatched grains served as an effective water permeation route. When sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) molecules stayed around the graphene membrane, the salt molecules near the entrance of the channel were significantly reduced [218]. In another research, a nanostructured coating (graphene nanoplatelets, GNPs) was introduced to the surface of the commercial polyethylene membrane for pilot-scale DCMD system. Over a 10-h operation, the membranes coated with 0.08 and 0.16 wt% GNP can reduce the membrane fouling by 59% and 78%, respectively [219].

It was confirmed that zero-dimensional nanomaterials as ultrasmall-size nanoparticles can enhance the membrane anti-fouling resistance. For example, Lei et al. synthesized functionalized hydrophobic C18-CQDs by selective grafting CQDs with ODA. By crosslinking the C18-CQDs with cotton fabric, the superhydrophobic membranes with high oil−water separation efficiency, high salt rejection rate (~99%) and anti-fouling performance were fabricated [220]. Furthermore, Jafari et al. investigated the potential of GQDs/PVDF nanofibrous membranes for AGMD. The membrane structure became more compact and rougher after adding GQDs. The membrane containing 0.25 wt% GQDs showed the permeate flux of 10.8 kg m−2 h−1 and salt rejection of 99.7% over 60 h filtration [221]. The incorporation of AC particles in polymer matrix was demonstrated to enhance the membrane permeability, because the intrinsic porous structure of AC particles could directly provide the transfer channels of water vapor in MD process. Zhao et al. fabricated porous membrane for vacuum membrane distillation (VMD) application by introducing macroporous AC into PVDF polymer matrix. After doping 0.09 wt% AC particles, the VMD flux increased from 42.0 kg m−2 h−1 for the pure PVDF membrane to 54.9 kg m−2 h−1 for the composite membrane [222]. Subsequently, they fabricated AC enhanced hydrophobic/hydrophilic dual-layer nanofiber composite membranes by electrospinning. Chua et al. formatted mesoporous carbon– silica nanocomposite (CSN) membranes by tri-constituent co-assembly method. The microstructure of the prepared membranes was characterized as highly ordered porous structure, moderate specific surface area (430–550 m2 g−1) and narrow pore size distribution (5.5–7.6 nm). The VMD performances of the CSN membranes were water permeation flux of 12 L m−2 h−1 and salt rejection over 99% [223].

The most commonly used materials in MD include the stretched polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), polyethylene (PE) membranes, the phase inverted PVDF and polypropylene (PP) membranes [196]. By incorporating a small amount of carbon nanomaterials into the polymer matrix, the water flux of the membrane can be significantly increased with the salt rejection higher than 95%. Information about the usages of carbon based nanomaterials in the fabrication of the MD membranes was shown in Table 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pervaporation (PV) membranes with carbon based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of a PV membrane is generally evaluated according to its productivity and capability to separate the components from the feed mixture [239]. The performance is typically characterized by flux (J) and separation factor (β) as defined by the following equations:
(1)
J =
Q
At
(2)
β
i / j
=
y
i
/
y
j
x
i
/
x
j
Where Q is the total mass transferred over time t, A is the membrane area, xi and yi are the molar fractions of one component in the feed and permeate, respectively, and xj and yj are the molar fractions of the other component in the feed and permeate, respectively [240].

The reports involved with the application of CNTs in PV membranes are very less. Until recently, Yang et al. produced PVA based MMMs containing MWCNTs via interfacial adhesion, hydrogen bonding and covalent bonding. The MWCNTs were well dispersed uniformly in PVA matrix, while MWCNTs without functional groups were easy to aggregate. A simplified schematic illustration is proposed in Fig. 6 for demonstrating water permeating through the membrane using NaCl solution as feed. The best desalination performance incorporating the 99.91% of salt rejection and 6.96 kg m−2 h−1 of water flux was obtained from the PVA/C-MWCNT/MA membrane using 35,000 ppm NaCl feed solution at room temperature. The PVA/C-MWCNT/MA membrane shows excellent stability on water flux and salt rejection over a 30 h durability test during processing the seawater collected from Brighton Beach [241].

Several research groups have confirmed that the GO-membrane based PV process showed great prospects for desalting applications. In GO-based membranes, the cross-linked cations in the GO nanochannels created accessible corridors for water molecules transmission meanwhile blocked other cations like Na+. With the increase of temperature, the amount of water permeation increased, while the migration of cations remained minimal. This phenomenon was not been observed in the non-thermal driven GO desalination membrane [242]. GO membranes showed a much higher pure water flux in PV compared to pressure-driven membrane. The pure water flux was found to be 1–2 orders of magnitude higher in PV due to the large capillary pressure induced by evaporation [243]. Feng's team fabricated GO based composite membranes via vacuum filtration, phase inversion and coaxial two-capillary spinning strategy, which possess improved performance in water permeability and separation in PV desalting process [244–246]. The optimal performances of GO/polyimide MMMs were a high permeate flux of 36.1 kg m−2 h−1 and a high salt rejection of about 99.9% at 90 ℃ [245]. It is very essential to tune the interlayer spacing of GO membranes in swelling state to improve the separation performance of PV. The commonly used crosslinking agents can be divided into two categories according to their molecular weight: small molecule crosslinking agents and polymer crosslinking agents [247]. Yang et al. employed hydrophilic sulfosuccinic acid (SSA) to interlink the GO nanosheets through an esterification reaction, leading to a restricted swelling of the graphene oxide framework (GOF)–SSA membrane. The swollen GOF–SSA membrane had a reduced nanochannel size of ≈0.59 nm, much smaller than that of the control GO membrane (≈0.90 nm) [248]. Yang et al. prepared thiourea covalent-linked GO framework membranes on porous ceramics through hydrothermal self-assembly synthesis. The small dimension of the covalent thiourea-link and the deoxygenated processes lead to the formation of narrow 2D channels in GO laminates. The rejections for all salts were higher than 99.8% with water fluxes in the range of 0.8–1.7 kg m−2 h−1 [249]. Song et al. proposed to use primary amine-terminated polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimers as crosslinkers to adjust the interlayer spacing of GO based membranes. The interlayer distance was tuned in the range of 0.43–0.76 nm under low addition amounts of dendrimers. The results showed that the permeation flux of GO based membrane is 124 kg m−2 h−1 and the rejection rate of NaCl is more than 99.99% under the simulated desalination conditions [247].

Whereas an alternative function of crosslinkers is enlarging the spacing distance between GO nanosheets for increasing the permeance of GO membranes. Qian et al. used cyclohyxanediamine (CDA) and p-phenylenediamine (pPDA) as crosslinker to prepare three dimensional GOF. the CDA-based graphene oxide composite membranes (GOCM) showed a higher water flux (20.1 kg m−2 h−1) with ion rejection of 99.9% than that of GO membrane (11.4 kg m−2 h−1) [250]. It attributes to the increase of interlayer spacing and improvement of surface hydrophilicity after modification of GO, Additionally, Sun et al. intercalated PVA chains into GO layers as the spacer to adjust the interlayer spacing and as the connector to interlock GO layers with the help of glutaraldehyde (GA) cross-linker. By appropriately tuning PVA intercalation, the interlayer spacing and cross-linking network structure were effectively tailored. The resultant c-GO-PVA10 membrane displayed high water flux (98.1 kg m−2 h−1) and high salt rejection (99.99%) for treating 10 wt% NaCl solution at 85 ℃ [251]. Cheng et al. developed a TFN composite membrane with the robust GO barrier layer. The GO layer was sturdily integrated onto the PAN nanofibrous support layer with the aid of PVA as a flexible connector and GA as a crosslinking agent. The resultant optimized TFN membrane displayed a relatively high permeate flux of 69.1 L m−2 h−1 and a stable high rejection rate of 99.9% for 24 h [252].

One major issue on the thermally-driven desalting membrane is scaling resulting in low permeate flux. Cha-Umpong et al. stacked deposited GO sheets on the PP hydrophobic hollow fiber membrane by vacuum filtration. The GO/PP membrane offered greater resistance to salt crystallization than the PP membrane due to the smooth, hydrophilic and negatively charged surface, which strengthen the electrostatic repulsion and energy barrier for heterogeneous nucleation. After two cleaning cycles, the desalination performance of the GO/PP composite membranes was still much better than that of the PP membrane [253]. In addition, GO based PV membrane is stable under high-salinity environment and long-term operation conditions in many studies [254–256].

Information about the usage of carbon based nanomaterials in PV membranes has been shown in Table 6. The desalination performances of various thermal-driven carbon based desalination membranes are displayed in Fig. 7. The comparison of rejection performance vs. the permeate flux of some MD and PV membranes can be directly observed.

Recent advances in carbon based nanomaterials and composite membranes have offered significant chances for the development of the water-treatment membranes. The essential properties of carbon nanomaterials including excellent mechanical characteristics, large specific surface area and rich modification approaches make them irreplaceable for the fabrication of the non-polyamide membranes. Through manipulating chemical structure, morphology and size of carbon nanomaterials, non-polyamide desalting membranes have been endowed with many preferable properties. As can be seen from this review, carbon nanomaterials have been extensively studied as self-supported membrane materials or as added components in polymer composites. By incorporating single or hydride carbon based nanomaterials into the non-polyamide membrane matrix, higher water flux and salt rejection have been obtained compared to the conventional membrane. This would be helpful to overcome the “trade-off” effect between membrane selectivity and water permeability. The non-polyamide membrane with an appropriate amount of carbon based nanomaterials possesses a decreased ICP resulting in the improvement of anti-fouling and chlorine resistance. In spite of great advances in the application of carbon materials in membranes, challenges are also encountered, such as the reliability, cost, regenerability, safety and scale-up applications. The following aspects should be focused on in future investigations.

Primarily, since the intrinsic physicochemical properties of carbon nanomaterials are different from each other, selecting proper carbon nanomaterials and precisely controlling their physicochemical properties to meet the optimal requirements and to demonstrate their full potential is very important. For instance, vertically-aligned MWCNTs are quite better options for improving the salt rejection and permeability compared with SWCNTs, due to the former having high hydrophobicity and the short path for water transport. GO nanosheet is a promising material for fabricating non-polyamide desalting membrane. However, more attention needs to be paid to its potential drawbacks, such as mechanical instability, unideal alignment and assembly, and surface defects. Moreover, the ideal interlayer spacing should be controlled in an accurate range for sieving the hydrated salt ions aswellas allowing water molecules to transport. Let alone the inevitably swelling of GO nanosheets in ionic solution is intractable for precise control of the interlayer spacing. The long term performance of the membrane is insufficiently considered in the previous studies. The longstanding tests including cycle cleaning and drop performance are required in the future to evaluate the life span of the carbon based membranes. It is still a challenge to produce ideal desalting non-polyamide membranes with desirable performance containing anti-fouling properties, chemical resistance, high mechanical strength high flux and selectivity. Some studies revealed that although flux was increased compared to conventional membranes, the salt rejection remained considerably low. The quest in exploring next-generation desalting membranes should be focused on not only enhancing the permeability while maintaining a satisfactory salt rejection, but also possessing better resistance to chlorine and fouling, along with cleanability and excellent mechanical properties and lifespan by combining the advantages between carbon based nanomaterials and non-polyamide membranes.

Some novel non-polyamide membranes have been successfully fabricated for improving desalting performance at the laboratory scale. However, few studies have been reported on upscaling the small-area membranes to low-cost, industrial-scale modules. Some of the carbon based nanomaterials desalting membranes are still in the theoretical research stage up to now. For instance, large scale assembly of highly dense vertical arrays of CNTs related membrane is a difficult issue. Although there are many reports on CNTs and/or graphene containing non-polyamide membranes in laboratory-scale, the commercial applications are ongoing in a low pace. It maybe attributes to the high cost of synthesizing CNTs and graphene-based materials, and the reproducibility and feasibility of the methods for making membranes are still in doubt. The technology of reliable production of carbon based nanomaterials free-standing membranes with sufficient mechanical strength has not been fully developed. The selling price was reported as ~$250 of a single sheet of monolayer graphene [259], which is higher than that of commercial RO membranes in the range of $20–40/m2 [260]. Nevertheless, it should be noticed that the high permeability of carbon nanomaterials based membrane may provide high water flux and small purification unit. And the better fouling/chlorine resistance offered by carbon nanomaterials based membranes can improve the reusability. All these characteristics could ultimately lower desalination costs. In addition, it is essential for scientists to develop the carbon nanomaterials based membranes with the perspective of industrial applications. Developing large-scale production of carbon based free-standing and composite membranes, exploring the feasible preparation technology, improving the mechanical property and durability, and realizing their utilization in seawater desalination, should be the focus of the future researches.

Owing to the issue on the structural stability of the nanocomposite membranes, leaching or peeling of the carbon nanomaterials from membranes into the retentate and/or permeate is inevitable. This process not only reduces the desalting performances and long-term durability of the membrane, but also causes some environmental problems. With the increase of research in commercial production and application, nanomaterials will be released to the environment at significant levels. Such exposure may cause toxic impacts to humans, animals and the natural environment. For example, CNTs and CQDs are responsible for the harmful damage to biological organisms because of their potential toxic effects. Much attention has been paid to research the consequences of different carbon nanomaterials on the natural environment recently. Nevertheless, up to now, no comprehensive investigation has been carried out to evaluate the leaching of nanoparticles in the long-term filtration process. Therefore, it is of great significance to improve the synthesis process of the carbon based membranes and standardize the toxicity evaluation method of carbon nanomaterials. Future research should focus on developing more effective approaches for anchoring carbon nanomaterials firmly to avoid the leaching by covalent bonding, coordination bonding and electrostatic interaction, etc. On the other hand, the strategies to monitor the leaching of carbon based materials should be included referring to leaching rate and time, along with a reasonable assessment of environment and human risk to quantify the correlation between their toxicity and exposure.

Summarily, nanotechnology is leading the way in developing desalination membranes for water purification, especially non-polyamide membranes. Despite the presence of the technical and scientific problem in the non-polyamide-based membranes, it is highly feasible that carbon nanomaterials incorporated membranes will be commercialized and industrialized in water purification and desalination fields in the near future. The contents herein provide an important insight into the comprehensive understanding of carbon nanomaterials utilized in desalination membranes. This piece of work is prospected to offer some clues for developing advanced desalination membranes by summarizing the research progress of non-polyamide membranes incorporated with carbon nanomaterials.

Qiang Li and Yi Wang wrote the original draft and edited the manuscript. Gongwen Tang and Na Zhang collected literature resources. Yi Wang reviewed and supervised the manuscript. All authors contributed to the writing process. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.
